region_of_origin: China (Camellia sinensis var. sinensis); widely consumed in Singapore, Japan, and across East and Southeast Asia
food_subgroup: Hot tea, brewed, unfermented, no milk, no sugar; 200ml serving; very low calorie (~2 kcal); moderate caffeine ~28–35mg per cup

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for brewed green tea beverage; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

serving.description: 1 cup = 200ml brewed, plain (no sugar, no milk)
serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is 1 cup (200ml) of brewed plain green tea with no sugar and no milk, equivalent to approximately 200g.

nutrient_source.title: Tea, hot, leaf, green — USDA SR Legacy (FDC ID 171917)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Direct match to USDA SR Legacy entry FDC ID 171917 'Tea, hot, leaf, green'. Values reported per 100g brewed beverage. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water are not reported in this USDA SR Legacy entry and are coded -1 to indicate not-reported rather than zero.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of beverages and foods with negligible carbohydrate content — University of Sydney GI Database consensus and peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.source_match_notes: Plain brewed green tea contains approximately 0.3g carbohydrate per 100g (0.6g per 200ml serving). No direct GI measurement exists for plain brewed green tea in the University of Sydney GI Database because the carbohydrate content is too low to elicit a measurable glycaemic response. GI is assigned as 1 (effectively zero) consistent with the University of Sydney convention for foods with negligible available carbohydrate. Glycaemic load per 200ml serving = (1 × 0.6) / 100 = 0.006, rounded to 0.0. This is the standard treatment for near-zero-carbohydrate beverages in the GI literature.

hypertension.guidance: Plain brewed green tea contributes only ~2mg sodium per 200ml serving, which is nutritionally negligible for blood pressure management; caffeine content (~30mg per cup) is low and unlikely to cause clinically significant acute BP elevation; meta-analyses of habitual green tea consumption (≥3 cups/day) report modest reductions in systolic BP of approximately 1–2 mmHg, suggesting a directionally favourable long-term effect.

type2_diabetes.guidance: Plain brewed green tea has a glycaemic index of approximately 1 and contributes zero added sugar; catechins, particularly EGCG (epigallocatechin-3-gallate, ~50–100mg per cup at standard brewing), modestly attenuate postprandial glucose rise via inhibition of intestinal alpha-amylase and SGLT1; meta-analyses associate habitual consumption of 3–5 cups/day with approximately 0.3 mmol/L lower fasting plasma glucose; freely recommended for people with Type 2 Diabetes when prepared without sugar or milk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, EGCG antioxidant activity
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain brewed green tea contains 0.0g saturated fat and 0.0mg cholesterol per 100g; habitual consumption of 3–5 cups/day is associated in meta-analyses with modest LDL-cholesterol reduction (Grade B evidence) and reduced oxidative modification of LDL via EGCG; it is considered a cardioprotective beverage choice when consumed without sugar or full-fat milk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Green Tea, Brewed, Plain (200ml) is an unfermented hot beverage prepared by steeping dried leaves of Camellia sinensis var. sinensis in water at 75–80°C for 2–3 minutes, either from loose-leaf or a teabag. The resulting infusion is pale yellow-green to light green in colour with a fresh, grassy, mildly astringent flavour and a clean, slightly vegetal aroma. When prepared without sugar or milk, a 200ml cup contains approximately 2 kcal, 0g fat, 0g sugar, 0.6g carbohydrate, and 2mg sodium — nutritionally negligible across all macronutrients. Caffeine content is approximately 28–35mg per 200ml cup, substantially lower than coffee (~95mg) or black tea (~47mg). The primary bioactive compound is EGCG (epigallocatechin-3-gallate), present at approximately 50–100mg per cup under standard brewing conditions; EGCG is a potent polyphenol antioxidant with documented anti-inflammatory (NF-κB pathway inhibition), antidiabetic (SGLT1 and alpha-amylase inhibition), and cardioprotective (LDL oxidation reduction) properties. Glycaemic index is effectively zero (assigned 1 by convention) due to negligible available carbohydrate. Widely consumed in Singapore Chinese households, Japanese chain cafés (sencha, gyokuro), and across East and Southeast Asia. Common varieties include sencha (Japan), longjing/dragonwell (China), and jasmine green tea (China/Singapore). CAUTION: bottled sweetened green tea drinks sold in Singapore convenience stores typically contain 10–25g added sugar per 250–500ml bottle and are nutritionally distinct from plain brewed green tea. Source: USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 171917. Confidence: high.
common_user_queries: is green tea good for weight loss

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 171917 (Tea, hot, leaf, green), which is an exact match for plain brewed green tea. GI assigned by convention for negligible-carbohydrate beverages consistent with University of Sydney GI Database methodology. Ontology codes verified against FoodOn and FoodEx2 published vocabularies.
notes: Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water content are coded -1 because these values are not reported in USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 171917 for this entry; they are not confirmed zero. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed trace/negligible per USDA). This record covers plain brewed green tea only; sweetened, bottled, or milk-added variants are nutritionally distinct and require separate records. Brewing temperature (75–80°C) and steep time (2–3 minutes) are recommended for maximum EGCG yield while minimising bitterness.
